perturbation.original_text: The Provider will agree to use their best efforts to prevent disclosure of Confidential Information to any third party for an indefinite period of time from the date of this Agreement. The Provider is to use best practice security measures at all times to prevent informati on from being compromised. All policies and procedures relating to data and information security are to be readily available to the Town from the Provider.
perturbation.changed_text: The Provider will endeavor to use reasonable efforts to prevent the unreasonable disclosure of Confidential Information to third parties for an indefinite period of time from the date of this Agreement. The Provider is to use generally accepted security measures at all times to prevent informati on from being compromised. Policies and procedures relating to data and information security will be made available to the Town from the Provider upon request.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates 'best efforts' and 'best practice security measures'. The modified text weakens these obligations to 'reasonable efforts' and 'generally accepted security measures', creating ambiguity and potentially undermining the required level of data protection. Also, by adding 'unreasonable' to the disclosure of information, the contract becomes even more open to interpretation. Furthermore, it states that policies and procedures will be made available 'upon request', instead of readily available. This change introduces ambiguity regarding the security measures' effectiveness and the accessibility of relevant documentation.
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA requires organizations to protect personal information with security safeguards appropriate to the sensitivity of the information. By changing the obligation to 'reasonable efforts' and 'generally accepted security measures', the provider might implement safeguards that are not sufficiently robust for the sensitivity of the data, violating PIPEDA's requirements for adequate protection. Also, by making policies and procedures available only upon request, it could be argued that the Town of Blue Mountains does not have sufficient access to the security measures being used by the Provider.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: PIPEDA requirements in brief - Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada Skip to main content Skip to "About this site" PIPEDA requirements in brief The Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act ( PIPEDA ) sets the ground rules for how private-sector organizations collect, use, and disclose personal information in the course of for-profit, commercial activities across Canada. PIPEDA also applies to the personal information of employees of federally-regulated businesses. On this page Key responsibilities under PIPEDA The 10 principles Does the Act apply to your business? Provincial privacy laws Information that crosses borders Federally regulated organizations What is personal information? What is not covered by PIPEDA ? Key responsibilities under PIPEDA Businesses that are subject to the Act must follow these 10 fair information principles to protect personal information, which are set out in Schedule 1 of PIPEDA . By following these principles, you will contribute to building trust in your business and in the digital economy. The 10 principles Accountability Identifying purposes Consent Limiting collection Limiting use, disclosure, and retention Accuracy Safeguards Openness Individual access Challenging compliance Read more about how to comply with these 10 principles in your organization. Does the Act apply to your business? PIPEDA applies to private-sector organizations across Canada that collect, use, or disclose personal information in the course of a commercial activity . The law defines a commercial activity as any particular transaction, act, or conduct, or any regular course of conduct that is of a commercial character, including the selling, bartering or leasing of donor, membership or other fundraising lists. Provincial privacy laws Alberta , British Columbia and Quebec have their own private-sector privacy laws that have been deemed substantially similar to PIPEDA . Organizations that are subject to a substantially similar provincial privacy law are generally exempt from PIPEDA with respect to the collection, use, or disclosure of personal information that occurs within that province. Ontario, New Brunswick, Nova Scotia and Newfoundland and Labrador have also adopted substantially similar legislation regarding the collection, use, and disclosure of personal health information. Information that crosses borders All businesses that operate in Canada and handle personal information that crosses provincial or national borders in the course of commercial activities are subject to PIPEDA , regardless of the province or territory in which they are based (including provinces with substantially similar legislation). Federally regulated organizations Federally regulated organizations that conduct business in Canada are always subject to PIPEDA . The Act also applies to their employees’ personal information. These organizations include: airports, aircraft and airl
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that undermine the level of data protection required by privacy laws like PIPEDA. The original contract mandates 'best efforts' and 'best practice security measures,' while the changed text weakens these obligations to 'reasonable efforts' and 'generally accepted security measures.' The Law Explanation states that PIPEDA requires organizations to protect personal information with security safeguards appropriate to the sensitivity of the information. Also, by making policies and procedures available only upon request, it could be argued that the Town of Blue Mountains does not have sufficient access to the security measures being used by the Provider. The snippet provided discusses PIPEDA requirements and states that businesses must follow fair information principles to protect personal information. By weakening the obligation to protect information, the modified contract creates ambiguity regarding the security measures' effectiveness and the accessibility of relevant documentation, potentially violating PIPEDA's requirements for adequate protection, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Provider shall immediately notify the Town of any security breach (including any internal unauthorized use or disclosure), investigate the security breach, and take measures to reme diate such breach at the Vendor s cost as directed by the Town.
perturbation.changed_text: The Provider shall, when practical, notify the Town of any potential security breach. The Provider will investigate such potential security breach, and may take measures to remediate such breach at the Vendor's cost.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall immediately notify' to 'shall, when practical, notify' introduces ambiguity and weakens the mandatory reporting requirement. Additionally, changing 'take measures to remediate' to 'may take measures to remediate' makes remediation discretionary, potentially delaying or preventing necessary action in response to a security breach. Internal unauthorized use or disclosure is no longer explicitly mentioned, which could be interpreted as meaning the provider does not have to report such cases. This undermines the required level of data protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: PIPEDA - Principle 4.7.3: Notification of security breaches
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA mandates that organizations must notify affected individuals and the Privacy Commissioner of Canada of any breach of security safeguards involving personal information that poses a real risk of significant harm to individuals. By making breach notification discretionary ('when practical') and remediation also discretionary ('may take measures'), the modified text allows the Provider to avoid fulfilling its legal obligations under PIPEDA to report and address security breaches promptly and effectively, which is crucial for mitigating harm to individuals.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: PIPEDA legislation and related regulations - Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada Skip to main content Skip to "About this site" PIPEDA legislation and related regulations About this legislation The Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA) is the federal privacy law for private-sector organizations. It sets out the ground rules for how businesses must handle personal information in the course of their commercial activity. For more information about the application of PIPEDA, please read our PIPEDA in brief page . Schedule 1 of PIPEDA contains 10 fair information principles . These principles are referred to throughout the Act. Administration Privacy Commissioner of Canada — The Office oversees compliance with PIPEDA. Canadians may complain to the Commissioner about any matter specified in section 11 of PIPEDA . The Commissioner may also personally initiate a complaint. Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada (ISED) - The Minister of ISED is responsible for PIPEDA. The minister recommends to the Governor in Council the adoption of Orders in Council under the Act and tables Government bills in Parliament to amend the Act. Related acts and legislation Several provincial laws have been deemed substantially similar to the PIPEDA . Under paragraph 26(2)(b) , the Governor in Council can exempt an organization or class of organizations, an activity or a class of activities from PIPEDA if the collection, use or disclosure of personal information occurs within a province that has legislation that has been deemed substantially similar to the PIPEDA. This means that wherever the substantially similar provincial law applies, that law applies instead of PIPEDA. Provincial laws that may apply instead of PIPEDA Canada's anti-spam legislation The Digital Privacy Act and PIPEDA Related topics Overview of privacy legislation in Canada Investigations into businesses PIPEDA interpretation bulletins The Application of PIPEDA to Charitable and Non-Profit Organizations The Application of PIPEDA to Municipalities, Universities, Schools, and Hospitals Leading by Example: Key developments in the first seven years of the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act Additional help Guidance documents to help you comply with PIPEDA PIPEDA interpretation bulletins Privacy Guide for Businesses Contact the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada Enforcement information Applying paragraphs 7(3)(d.1) and 7(3)(d.2) of PIPEDA How PIPEDA applies to the creation and use of electronic docume
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the principles of PIPEDA, a federal privacy law. The original contract mandates immediate notification and remediation of security breaches. The modified version introduces ambiguity by stating notification is required 'when practical' and remediation 'may' be taken. This contradicts the underlying purpose of PIPEDA, which, according to the provided snippet, 'sets out the ground rules for how businesses must handle personal information in the course of their commercial activity.' The modification weakens the Provider's obligation to protect personal information, allowing for delayed notification and potentially no remediation, directly undermining PIPEDA's intent.

perturbation.original_text: The Town retains the right to audit the Provider to ensure that proper measures are being applied to protect any and all confidential information. Auditing may be performed by Town IT Staff or by a Third Par ty, as chosen by the Town in its sole discretion.
perturbation.changed_text: The Town may, at its convenience, conduct a cursory review of the Provider's general security practices. Such review may be performed by the Town, subject to the Provider's reasonable approval.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a clear and unfettered right for the Town to audit the Provider. The modified text weakens this right by changing 'retains the right to audit' to 'may, at its convenience, conduct a cursory review', making the review optional and less thorough. The addition of 'general security practices' limits the scope of the review. Furthermore, adding 'subject to the Provider's reasonable approval' introduces a significant limitation on the Town's ability to conduct the review, as the Provider can now potentially block or delay the audit.
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA requires organizations to be accountable for personal information under their control. This includes implementing policies and practices to give effect to the principles, including openness about policies and practices relating to the management of personal information. The change limits the Town's ability to verify the Provider's compliance with PIPEDA's requirements through an effective audit, thus hindering the Town's ability to ensure accountability for personal information. The 'reasonable approval' aspect may make it impossible for the Town to do an audit that would truly ensure compliance.

perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the Town's right to audit the Provider, potentially hindering its ability to ensure accountability for personal information as required by PIPEDA. The original text grants the Town an unfettered right to audit, whereas the changed text introduces limitations: the review is now 'cursory', focuses on 'general security practices', and requires the 'Provider's reasonable approval'. The Law Explanation states that the 'reasonable approval' aspect may make it impossible for the Town to do an audit that would truly ensure compliance.' The scraped snippet mentions that PIPEDA 'sets out the ground rules for how businesses must handle personal information in the course of their commercial activity' and that 'The Office oversees compliance with PIPEDA'. The changes to the text introduce ambiguity and limitations that contradict the intent of PIPEDA.
